hero.title: Welcome to Lingoix
hero.subtitle: The best platform for learning languages
hero.heading: Learn languages faster with AI!
hero.subheading: Master any language with personalized AI-powered lessons and a proven learning roadmap.

productSection.title: A learning system built around evidence
productSection.subtitle: Lingoix connects resources, practice, learner performance, and teacher review into one adaptive learning workflow.

path.title: Learning Path
path.description: Learners follow an adjustable path shaped by goals, CEFR level, practice results, and targeted exercise insertions.

profile.title: Performance Profile
profile.description: Vocabulary, grammar, listening, reading, writing, translation, and speaking are visible in one language profile.

resources.title: Resource Library
resources.description: Books, audio, grammar notes, vocabulary lists, and conversation topics support the learner's path.

exercises.title: Exercise Bank
exercises.description: Structured exercises collect Learning Events through flashcards, choices, listening, writing, and conversation practice.

review.title: Teacher Review
review.description: Teachers and admins review proposed adaptive decisions with evidence before targeted exercises are applied.

conversation.title: Conversation Practice
conversation.description: Topic-based prompts build speaking ability now, with pronunciation analysis ready to become a later layer.

steps.2: Complete your profile and personalize your experience

description.1: From the beginning, please specify how you want to use this platform: • Student: Learn the language and track progress • Teacher: Teach and manage students • School: Manage classes and student performance
description.2: Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit, sed do eiusmod tempor incididunt ut labore et dolore magna aliqua. Ut enim ad minim veniam, quis nostrud exercitation ullamco laboris nisi ut aliquip ex ea commodo consequat.
description.3: Description: This is your control center! Depending on the role you choose, you can: • Student: Follow the learning roadmap, tests, and exercises. • Teacher: See student progress and assign assignments. • School: Manage student and teacher performance.
description.4: Now it's your turn to take the first step! • Student: View a sample lesson and take a mini-test. • Teacher: Create a test assignment. • School: View a sample student report.
description.5: You've learned about the basic features of Lingoix. Now you can get started and use this platform for learning, teaching, and managing classes.

research.title: Adaptive Learning Research Results
research.subtitle: Model evaluation, statistical analysis, adaptive decisions, and generated thesis artifacts from the reproducible Python pipeline.
research.loadErrorTitle: Research outputs could not be loaded.
research.reportLanguageNote: The generated academic report is currently shown in English; the page controls and research tables follow the selected interface language.

modelSelector.title: Model selector
modelSelector.description: Choose which model family to inspect. This filters generated research outputs only; it does not apply adaptive decisions to learner paths.

overview.title: Compare the research pipeline
overview.body: Overview mode keeps classification, regression, clustering, statistical, weakness, and rule-decision outputs together so admins can scan the whole synthetic-data pipeline.

classification.title: Weakness classification view
classification.body: Classifier models estimate which injected weakness category a synthetic learner belongs to. Feature importance helps explain which simulated signals shaped those classifications.

regression.title: Outcome regression view
regression.body: Regression models estimate simulated mastery outcomes. Statistical tests sit beside them so the outcome comparison remains framed as synthetic research, not learner proof.

clustering.title: Learner archetype clustering view
clustering.body: Clustering models group synthetic learners by behavioral patterns. Archetype distribution explains the simulated learner mix behind those clusters.

archetypeSelector.title: Learner archetype selector
archetypeSelector.description: Focus the research outputs on one synthetic learner archetype. This narrows explanatory tables and sample decisions without changing product data.
archetypeSelector.allBody: The tables below show the complete synthetic learner mix, including generated archetypes, weakness categories, and sampled adaptive decisions.
archetypeSelector.selectedBody: Showing {{count}} synthetic learners in the {{archetype}} archetype, plus matching cluster assignments and sampled adaptive decisions where available.

generated.reviewContent: Review German {{skill}} items connected to repeated errors.
generated.nextStep: Insert {{exercise}} before the next mixed-skill lesson.

researchGuidance.title: How to Read the Adaptive Learning Research
researchGuidance.subtitle: A guided explanation of the synthetic dataset, machine-learning models, evaluation metrics, adaptive decisions, and the limits of what the results prove.

sections.title: Purpose of This Section
sections.body: This page explains the research prototype in human language. The results dashboard shows outputs; this guide explains why those outputs exist and how to interpret them.
points: The study uses synthetic learner data because real learner data is unavailable.
The goal is to test whether injected learning patterns can be recovered by machine-learning models.
The results are useful for thesis methodology and product direction, not proof of real-world learning effectiveness.

sections.title: Research Pipeline
sections.body: The pipeline starts by generating virtual learners, simulates hundreds of learning events per learner, engineers features, trains models, evaluates results, and writes the final report.
points: Raw interactions become features such as error rate, hint use, retry frequency, retention, and mastery proxy.
The same seed reproduces the same learners, tables, figures, and report.
Generated ground truth is kept separate from model-discovered patterns.

sections.title: Classification Models
sections.body: Classification models predict the learner weakness category. The important question is whether behavior features can identify vocabulary, grammar, reading, listening, or balanced learners.
points: Random Forest is strong for feature importance and nonlinear patterns.
Logistic Regression is simple, fast, and interpretable.
SVM can separate complex boundaries but is less transparent.
CatBoost, XGBoost, and LightGBM are gradient-boosting models when available.

sections.title: Regression Models
sections.body: Regression models estimate future simulated outcomes: future mastery proxy, future performance score, and next-session success probability.
points: RMSE and MAE show prediction error.
R² shows how much target variance the model explains.
Linear Regression can win when the target is smooth and feature relationships are close to linear.

sections.title: Clustering Models
sections.body: Clustering models discover learner groups without using the hidden labels. This helps test whether behavioral patterns naturally form groups similar to the injected archetypes.
points: K-Means is useful when groups are compact and roughly balanced.
Hierarchical clustering shows nested similarity structure.
DBSCAN searches for dense regions and may return few clusters when the dataset is small or diffuse.

sections.title: Adaptive Decisions
sections.body: The recommendation engine is framed as Lingoix Adaptive Decisions. It turns recent performance and error patterns into difficulty, exercise type, review content, and next learning-path suggestions.
points: Rule-based decisions are easier to audit and explain.
ML-based decisions can scale to richer patterns but need strong evidence presentation.
Adaptive decisions should be treated as proposed interventions until reviewed.

sections.title: How to Interpret the Tables
sections.body: High model scores mean the pipeline recovered patterns that were intentionally embedded in the synthetic simulation. They do not mean the system has been validated with real learners.
points: Use macro F1 to compare classifiers when classes are imbalanced.
Use RMSE and MAE to compare regressors.
Use Silhouette and Davies-Bouldin to compare cluster quality.
Use feature importance to explain which behaviors drive predictions.

sections.title: Threats to Validity
sections.body: The strongest honest claim is methodological: the project demonstrates an end-to-end adaptive-learning research pipeline when real learner data is unavailable.
points: Synthetic assumptions influence the results.
Labels are generated by the same simulation logic that creates behavior.
The mastery score is a proxy, not true CEFR mastery.
Real learner validation is future work.

rows.name: Random Forest
rows.purpose: Predict weakness categories and estimate future scores using many decision trees.
rows.interpretation: Strong baseline for nonlinear data; feature importance helps explain the prediction.

rows.name: CatBoost
rows.purpose: Gradient boosting classifier that often performs well on tabular data.
rows.interpretation: If it wins, the data likely benefits from boosted tree interactions.

rows.name: Logistic Regression
rows.interpretation: Useful as an interpretable baseline; strong results suggest simple feature boundaries.

rows.name: SVM
rows.purpose: Classifier that separates learners through a margin-based decision boundary.
rows.interpretation: Useful for complex boundaries, but harder to explain to nontechnical reviewers.

rows.name: Linear Regression
rows.purpose: Predict future numeric outcomes from engineered features.
rows.interpretation: A strong result suggests the simulated target is smooth and feature-driven.

rows.name: K-Means
rows.purpose: Group learners into a fixed number of clusters.
rows.interpretation: Best read as exploratory grouping, then compared with hidden archetypes.

steps.title: Recommended Reading Order
items: Start with Dataset Characteristics to understand what was generated.
Read Feature Engineering to see how interactions became model inputs.
Compare Classification Results to identify the best weakness detector.
Compare Regression Results to understand future-performance prediction.
Review Clustering Results to see unsupervised learner groups.
Inspect Adaptive Decisions to understand how model outputs become learning-path suggestions.
Finish with Threats to Validity before making research claims.
